behaviors.description: Request sent to external API includes complete list of valid taxonomy labels
behaviors.justification: This tests request construction. A real failure is that taxonomy labels are omitted from the request payload. A mock of the external API can inspect the call arguments to verify the labels are present. No integration with actual API infrastructure is needed to catch this logic error.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API, call the module with a test document, capture and inspect the request arguments passed to the mocked call, and assert that all taxonomy labels (legal, technical, financial, other) are present in the request payload.

behaviors.description: When API returns label outside taxonomy, module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding it
behaviors.justification: This tests output validation and error handling. A real failure is that unexpected API responses bypass the validation check and propagate invalid labels to callers. A mock API configured to return an invalid label can verify the module sanitizes it without needing a real API.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the mocked external API to return a classification label that is not in the fixed taxonomy, call the module with a test document, and assert that the module returns exactly "unknown" rather than forwarding the invalid label.

behaviors.description: Classification result for same document is identical across repeated calls
behaviors.justification: The real failure is either missing result caching (repeated API calls get different results due to non-determinism) or broken caching logic (wrong cache key). Both failures are detectable at Unit level by mocking an API that returns different values on each call and verifying the module returns the cached result on the second call. A secondary failure mode exists if the module relies on API determinism—testing that requires Agentic-level testing against a real LLM—but caching is the expected implementation.
behaviors.test_description: Call the module twice with identical document input. Mock the external API to return different classification labels on first and second calls (simulating API non-determinism). Assert that the module returns the same cached result on the second call, proving the caching layer is working.

behaviors.description: Number of tokens consumed stays within configured per-call budget
behaviors.justification: A real failure is that the module doesn't check the API's reported token usage against the budget, or the check is broken. The API response typically includes token usage metrics. A mock API returning various token counts (both within and exceeding budget) can verify the budget-checking logic without a real API. The failure is about the module's validation logic, not about actual token consumption variability.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the mocked API to return token usage counts both within and exceeding the configured budget. Call the module with a typical document and assert that it accepts results within budget and appropriately rejects or handles results exceeding the budget (e.g., raises an error, logs a warning, retries with smaller request).

behaviors.description: When API signals rate limit, module waits and retries instead of returning error
behaviors.justification: This behavior involves coordinating multiple components: detecting a rate-limit error, executing a wait, and retrying the request. The real failure is not just missing retry logic but also broken timing or coordination between steps. A pure in-process mock of the API response alone would miss the failure where the module retries immediately without waiting. Testing the wait-and-retry coordination requires either Integration-level testing with a stateful mock that tracks call sequencing and enforces timing, or Unit-level testing with time mocking infrastructure. Integration testing with a mock API that fails on first call(s) and succeeds on retry is the most straightforward approach.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock of the external API that returns a rate-limit error on the first call and a successful classification on the second call. Set a configured wait duration. Call the module with a test document and verify that it catches the rate-limit error, waits for the configured duration, retries the request, and returns the eventual successful classification result rather than immediately failing to the caller.
